title: Add Fractional Areas with Same Denominator
description: Combine fractional areas that share a common denominator by adding numerators and keeping the denominator.
condition: Two or more fractions with the same denominator to be added (e.g., partial areas from an area model).
response: Add the numerators and keep the common denominator; simplify if possible.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Applies when combining partial products in area models.

title: Add Volumes of Multiple Prisms
description: To find total volume of a composite solid made of rectangular prisms, add the volumes of the individual prisms.
condition: A solid is composed of two or more non-overlapping right rectangular prisms with known dimensions.
response: Compute each prism’s volume and add them to get the total volume.
label: Principle / Model
example: If one prism has volume $8 \times 4 \times 10 = 320$ and another has volume $4 \times 7 \times 2 = 56$, then total volume is $320 + 56 = 376$.
notes: In Q4, dimensions are arranged so overlapping is avoided by decomposing correctly.

title: Area of Composite Rectilinear Figure
description: Find the area of a composite figure by decomposing it into rectangles, finding each area, and combining appropriately.
condition: A rectilinear figure composed of rectangular parts with given side lengths.
response: Decompose into rectangles, compute each area (length × width), and add or subtract to find total area.
label: Principle / Model
example: Split the L-shape into two rectangles and compute each area, then add to get \(37\tfrac{3}{5}\).
notes: Assumes additivity of area.

title: Convert Mixed Number to Improper Fraction
description: Rewrite a mixed number as an improper fraction before multiplying.
condition: A mixed number $a\ \frac{b}{c}$ is given in a multiplication problem.
response: Compute $\frac{ac+b}{c}$.
label: Principle
notes: Standard prerequisite for fraction multiplication. [merged: condition varies] [merged: response varies] [merged: label varies]

title: Decompose Mixed Number for Area Model
description: Split a mixed number into whole and fractional parts to build an area model.
condition: A mixed number used in an area model representation.
response: Represent as sum of whole number and fraction.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Supports visual multiplication strategy.

title: Determine Common Unit Fraction for Tiling
description: Identify a unit fraction that evenly partitions both side lengths of a rectangle.
condition: Two fractional side lengths; need square tile with no gaps or overlaps.
response: Choose a unit fraction whose denominator is a common multiple of the side denominators.
label: Principle / Model
example: Sides $\frac{3}{8}$ and $\frac{2}{5}$ → common denominator $40$ → tile $\frac{1}{40}$ yard.
notes: Uses least common multiple reasoning.

title: Determine Number of Smaller Prisms by Dimension Ratios
description: Find how many smaller rectangular prisms fit inside a larger prism by dividing corresponding dimensions and multiplying the quotients.
condition: Given dimensions of a large rectangular prism and a smaller prism that tiles it exactly.
response: Divide each large dimension by the corresponding small dimension and multiply the three quotients.
label: Principle / Model
example:
Large box: 48 cm × 36 cm × 12 cm; Small box: 18 cm × 6 cm × 8 cm.

$$\frac{48}{18} = \frac{36}{6} = \frac{12}{8}$$ (after matching compatible dimensions appropriately) gives factors 2, 6, and 2.

$$2 \times 6 \times 2 = 24 \text{ small boxes.}$$
notes: Requires matching compatible dimensions; assumes exact tiling with no gaps.

title: Determine Volume by Counting Unit Cubes
description: Find the volume of a prism by counting the total number of unit cubes that fill it.
condition: A rectangular prism composed of visible unit cubes where each cube represents 1 cubic unit.
response: Count or compute the total number of unit cubes to determine volume.
label: Production / Skill
example:
If a prism has 7 cubes along the length, 6 along the width, and 4 along the height, then:

$$7 \times 6 \times 4 = 168 \text{ cubic units.}$$
notes: Often performed by structuring cubes into layers (arrays).

title: Identify Kite by Adjacent Equal Sides
description: Recognize that a quadrilateral with at least two pairs of adjacent sides of equal length is a kite.
condition: A quadrilateral has at least two pairs of adjacent sides marked congruent.
response: Classify the figure as a kite.
label: Concept
example: If a quadrilateral has sides $AB = BC$ and $CD = DA$, then it is a kite.
notes: Definition-based classification.

title: Identify Parallelogram by Parallel Sides
description: Recognize that a quadrilateral with two pairs of opposite sides parallel is a parallelogram.
condition: A quadrilateral shows two pairs of opposite sides parallel.
response: Classify the figure as a parallelogram.
label: Concept
example: If $AB \parallel CD$ and $BC \parallel AD$, then $ABCD$ is a parallelogram.
notes: Used when interpreting diagram markings.

title: Identify Quadrilateral by Four Sides
description: Recognize that any polygon with four sides is a quadrilateral.
condition: A polygon has four sides.
response: Classify the figure as a quadrilateral.
label: Concept
example: A polygon with vertices $A,B,C,D$ (4 sides) is a quadrilateral.
notes: Broadest category in the hierarchy.

title: Identify Rectangle by Four Right Angles
description: Recognize that a quadrilateral with four right angles is a rectangle.
condition: A quadrilateral has four right angle markers.
response: Classify the figure as a rectangle.
label: Concept
example: If all angles in $ABCD$ are $90^\circ$, then $ABCD$ is a rectangle.
notes: Side lengths may differ.

title: Identify Rhombus by Four Equal Sides
description: Recognize that a quadrilateral with all sides equal in length is a rhombus.
condition: A quadrilateral has four congruent sides.
response: Classify the figure as a rhombus.
label: Concept
example: If $AB = BC = CD = DA$, then $ABCD$ is a rhombus.
notes: Angles need not be right angles.

title: Identify Special Quadrilateral by Properties
description: Classify a quadrilateral (e.g., trapezoid, parallelogram, rectangle, kite) using properties like parallel sides, equal sides, or right angles.
condition: A quadrilateral with marked properties (parallel sides, congruent sides, right angles).
response: Select all shape names that match the given properties.
label: Rule / Plan
example: If both pairs of opposite sides are parallel → Parallelogram.
notes: Relies on definitions of each named figure.

title: Identify Square by Equal Sides and Right Angles
description: Recognize that a quadrilateral with four equal sides and four right angles is a square.
condition: A quadrilateral has four congruent sides and four right angles.
response: Classify the figure as a square.
label: Concept
example: If $AB = BC = CD = DA$ and each angle is $90^\circ$, then $ABCD$ is a square.
notes: Combines properties of rhombus and rectangle.

title: Identify Trapezoid by One Pair of Parallel Sides
description: Recognize that a quadrilateral with at least one pair of parallel sides is a trapezoid.
condition: A quadrilateral has at least one pair of opposite sides parallel.
response: Classify the figure as a trapezoid.
label: Concept
example: If $AB \parallel CD$ but $BC \nparallel AD$, then $ABCD$ is a trapezoid.
notes: Inclusive definition assumed (at least one pair).

title: Identify Volume as Attribute of Capacity
description: Volume describes how much space an object occupies or how much a container can hold.
condition: Asked which attribute describes how much substance (e.g., milk) a container holds.
response: Select or state 'volume.'
label: Concept
example: When asked how much milk is in a bottle, the correct attribute is $\text{volume}$.
notes: Distinguishes volume from width, height, or area.

title: Interpret Cubic Units as Volume
description: Recognize that the number of cubic units filling a solid represents its volume.
condition: A solid measured in cubic units (e.g., cubic centimeters, cubic inches, unit cubes).
response: State that the count of cubic units equals the volume of the solid.
label: Concept
example: If a prism contains 168 unit cubes, then its volume is 168 cubic units.
notes: Connects discrete cube counting to the concept of volume.

title: Match Prism Volume to Target Quantity
description: Determine whether a prism’s computed volume equals a specified target amount.
condition: Given a prism’s dimensions and a target volume (e.g., 16 milliliters).
response: Compute the prism’s volume and compare it to the target value; answer Yes if equal, otherwise No.
label: Principle / Model
example:
If a prism measures 4 cm × 2 cm × 2 cm, then:

$$V = 4 \times 2 \times 2 = 16 \text{ cm}^3,$$
so it can hold exactly 16 milliliters (Yes).
notes: Uses the fact that 1 cm³ = 1 mL.

title: Multiply Mixed Numbers
description: Multiply mixed numbers by converting to improper fractions and multiplying.
condition: Two mixed numbers are multiplied.
response: Convert each to improper fractions, multiply, and convert back if needed.
label: Principle
notes: Composite KC built from conversion + fraction multiplication.

title: Multiply Three Factors
description: Multiply three whole numbers to find a product.
condition: Given three numerical factors to combine multiplicatively.
response: Compute their product.
label: Production / Skill
notes: Arithmetic skill supporting volume computation.

title: Recognize Square as Rectangle and Rhombus
description: Understand that a square satisfies the definitions of both a rectangle and a rhombus.
condition: A figure is identified as a square.
response: Conclude it is also a rectangle and a rhombus.
label: Principle
example: Since a square has four right angles and four equal sides, it meets the definitions of both a rectangle and a rhombus.
notes: Explains why some rectangles are rhombuses and all squares are rhombuses.

title: Relate Cubic Centimeters to Milliliters
description: Recognize that 1 cubic centimeter is equivalent to 1 milliliter.
condition: Volume expressed in cubic centimeters for a container’s capacity.
response: Interpret the value as the same number of milliliters.
label: Fact
example:
$$1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1 \text{ mL.}$$

So 16 cm³ equals 16 mL.
notes: Metric volume equivalence.

title: Select Dimensions Matching Given Volume
description: Choose three whole-number dimensions whose product equals a given volume.
condition: Given a target volume and a list of possible dimension values.
response: Select three numbers whose product equals the target volume.
label: Production / Skill
example: For volume $45$, one valid set is $3 \times 3 \times 5 = 45$.
notes: Requires knowledge of factor combinations.

title: Simplify a Fraction
description: Reduce a fraction to lowest terms by dividing numerator and denominator by their greatest common factor.
condition: A fraction where numerator and denominator share a common factor greater than 1.
response: Divide numerator and denominator by the GCF.
label: Principle / Model
notes: Often final step after multiplication.

title: Solve for Missing Prism Dimension
description: Find an unknown dimension of a rectangular prism by dividing volume by the product of the known dimensions.
condition: Given volume and two dimensions of a rectangular prism, with one dimension unknown.
response: Divide volume by (known dimension × known dimension) to find the missing value.
label: Principle / Model
example: If $V = 756$, $h = 7$, $w = 18$, then length $= \frac{756}{7 \times 18} = 6$.
notes: Uses inverse relationship of multiplication and division.

title: Solve for Missing Prism Height Using Equal Volumes
description: Set volumes of two rectangular prisms equal and solve for the unknown height.
condition: Two prisms with equal volumes; dimensions of one known and one dimension missing in the other.
response: Compute known volume and divide by known base area to find missing height.
label: Principle / Model
example: Volume A: \(5\times12\times6=360\). For B: \(15\times4\times h=360\Rightarrow h=6\).
notes: Uses volume formula and division.

title: Total from (Amount per Batch) × (Number of Batches)
description: Multiply quantity per group by number of groups, then sum totals.
condition: Problem gives amount per batch and number of batches for multiple items.
response: Multiply each pair and add results.
label: Principle
notes: Multi-step word problem structure.
